I read the prompt as fixing the special tame-format presentation: first two variables are the tame generators ŌÉ,ŌĄ, the relation ŌĄŌÉ=ŌĄ2 is implicit, later generators lie in the pro-2 core, and profinite exponents are represented modulo M=85667662080.I treated the 67 prior turns as a proposal log rather than as proof; the only still-unrefuted word in that log is the ‚Äúdynamic tame-inertia‚ÄĚ candidate.

The live candidate remains:variables sigma,tau,x,y definitions nx = (x*tau^50932630783)^40491355905 ny = (y*tau)^40491355905 relations x*sigma^45176306175*x*sigma^40491355905*x^-1*nx*ny^sigma*y^-1

Equivalently, with ŌÄ=ŌÄ2‚ąąZ and ő∑=2ŌÄ on odd tame inertia,r=xŌÉ‚ąíŌÄxŌÉŌÄx‚ąí1(xŌĄ‚ąíő∑)ŌÄ ((yŌĄ)ŌÄ)ŌÉy‚ąí1,with ŌÄ‚Č°40491355905,‚ąíŌÄ‚Č°45176306175,‚ąíő∑‚Č°50932630783(modM).The 2-primary coordinate of ‚ąíő∑ is irrelevant in this occurrence because that exponent is applied only to ŌĄ, whose finite tame images have odd order.

Audit of what is solid Two equations should be frozen exactly as equations, not as informal fractions.For the sign-cover norm coefficient, let t be the deck involution of the sign double cover and let ŌĄ‚Ü¶őĪÓÄ†=1 be a nontrivial odd tame-inertia character. ForNa (u)=(uŌĄa)ŌÄ,the Fox coefficient isDa=j=0‚ąĎŌÄ‚ąí1(tőĪa)j,and it satisfies (1‚ąítőĪa)Da=1‚ąí(tőĪa)ŌÄ=1‚ąít.ThusDa=(1‚ąít)(1‚ąítőĪa)‚ąí1.This is the direction that makes the unit/radical argument valid: 1‚ąítőĪa is a 2-adic unit, 1‚ąít is radical, so Da is radical and ‚ąí1+D‚ąíő∑ is a unit. Hence the nontrivial odd tame-character sign-cover blocks contribute only unit Smith pivots.For the trivial tame-inertia block, with T the ŌÉ-cycle operator and s‚Č°ŌÄ(modf), the mod-2 linear relation is(1+T‚ąís)X+(1+T)Y=0 up to the harmless T‚ÜĒT‚ąí1 convention. The legitimate elimination is the polynomial Ps (T) defined by (1+T)Ps (T)=1+T‚ąís.

One must not invert 1+T in F2 [T]/(Tf‚ąí1). With h(T)=1+Ps (T)+Ps(T‚ąí1),the prior nonsingularity proof gives (1+ő∂)h(ő∂)=ő∂‚ąís+ő∂s+1=ő∂‚ąís(1+ő∂2s+1),so h has no nontrivial f-th-root zero because 2s+1 is coprime to f. With these conventions, the candidate-side conclusionsUe,fab‚ČÉZ2ef+1‚äēC2,(kerőļ)ab‚ČÉZ22ef+1‚äēC4 remain coherent.New Turn 68 contribution: a prime-power descent test for anti-cyclotomic residuals The late prior turns correctly reframed the remaining first-IA obstruction as an arithmetic-minus-candidate residual. For even f=2n, the mixed part depends only on d=gcd(e,2n+1),and is modeled byAd=F2[Cd]‚ČÉF2 [T]/(Td‚ąí1),plus the two universal square coordinates ŌĶb,ŌĶS.

Turn 63 introduced exact-order projectors Ec(d). I add a more directly usable coefficient test, especially for the prime-power cases that appear early in the priority list.

For c‚ą£d, define the folded residualŌĀc (R)(U)=i=0‚ąĎc‚ąí1 0‚Č§j<dj‚Č°i(modc)‚ąĎrjUi ifR(T)=j=0‚ąĎd‚ąí1rjTj.

Then the exact-order-c component of R vanishes if and only ifő¶c (U)‚ą£ŌĀc (R)(U)inF2[U].Reason: evaluating R on a primitive c-th root is exactly evaluating the folded polynomial ŌĀc (R); the primitive c-component is zero exactly when all primitive c-roots are zeros, i.e. when ő¶c divides ŌĀc (R). This avoids naming Wedderburn factors, so it is stable under the tame-generator gauge T‚Ü¶Tu and normal-basis unit gauges.

A useful special case is c=‚Ąďr, ‚Ąď odd prime. Sinceő¶‚Ąďr (U)=1+U‚Ąď r‚ąí1+U2‚Ąď r‚ąí1+‚čĮ+U (‚Ąď‚ąí1)‚Ąď r‚ąí1,the primitive ‚Ąďr -part vanishes exactly when the folded coefficients are constant on fibers modulo ‚Ąď r‚ąí1. Equivalently, for R‚ąąF2[C‚Ąďr],ri=r i+‚Ąďr‚ąí1=‚čĮ=r i+(‚Ąď‚ąí1)‚Ąďr‚ąí1 (0‚Č§i<‚Ąď r‚ąí1).

This recovers the prior c=9 conditionr0=r3=r6,r1=r4=r7,r2=r5=r8,but now it is not a one-off calculation. It is the general prime-power descent criterion.

Concrete consequences for the next arithmetic tests:

For c=3, the primitive part vanishes iff r0=r1=r2; full zero then requires the common value to be 0.For c=5, the primitive part vanishes iff all five coefficients are equal; full zero then requires that common value to be 0.For c=9, first test the block equalities above; if they hold, descend to the C3 -residual (a0,a1,a2) and test a0=a1=a2, then test the invariant component.

For c=11, because ő¶11 has degree 10, the primitive 11-part vanishes iff all eleven coefficients are equal; full zero requires that common value to be 0.The primitive visibility table from Turn 67 is correct, and the next entries after its displayed range are:c359111713331927254365294157n(c)123546599107614109 f=2n(c)24610812101818201412282018 ef=2cn(c)620541101361563303424865006027808128201026 So the clean arithmetic program remains c=3, c=5, c=9, c=11, c=17, c=13, ‚Ä¶; the c=29 and c=41 tests enter soon after c=65, before some larger composite cases.This does not compute any arithmetic residual. It only makes the residual test more explicit and gauge-stable.

ESTABLISHED: The dynamic candidate remains the only unrefuted candidate. The stable equations areDa=(1‚ąít)(1‚ąítőĪa)‚ąí1,(1+T)Ps (T)=1+T‚ąís.

I added a coefficient-level exact-order test: for R‚ąąF2[Cd], the exact-order-c component vanishes iff ő¶c (U)‚ą£ŌĀc (R)(U). In particular, for c=‚Ąďr, primitive ‚Ąďr -vanishing is exactly block-constancy of coefficients along fibers modulo ‚Ąď r‚ąí1.

GAP: No arithmetic-minus-candidate residual has been computed. The presentation is therefore still not proved. The missing step is either a dyadic formation theorem or explicit Kummer/Witt arithmetic showing that the true filtered tame action has zero residual in every primitive anti-cyclotomic component.

NEXT: Compute the normalized arithmetic residuals in order c=3,5,9,11. For c=9, first apply the block-constancy test ri=ri+3=ri+6, then descend to c=3. For c=11, test whether all eleven mixed coefficients are equal, then whether the invariant value is zero.[BREAKTHROUGH]
